video_title: NY Governor Kathy Hochul on Her One Year Data Center Moratorium | Odd Lots
gist: New York Governor Kathy Hochul explains the state's new one-year data center moratorium as a negotiating pause requiring developers to bring their own power or pay a grid premium, details an 'all of the above' energy buildout (nuclear, Quebec hydro, offshore wind, gas) aimed at winning jobs-heavy investments like Micron's $100 billion fab, and discusses managing AI-driven labor disruption via a paused Waymo trial, an AI-assisted regulatory purge, and housing-cost politics behind New York's leftward electoral shift.

insights.claim: New York became the first U.S. state to impose a formal one-year moratorium on new large-scale data centers, which Governor Kathy Hochul frames as a pause to set clear rules rather than an outright ban, triggered by a sudden surge of siting proposals.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Sets a template other states may copy; data center developers and their suppliers (power, cooling, chips) face at least a year of regulatory uncertainty in the largest East Coast market.
insights.quote: It is not a ban. It is simply saying, step back, take a breath and get it right.

insights.claim: A single data center project of just 50 megawatts draws power equivalent to roughly 50,000 homes, illustrating why even mid-sized AI data centers strain New York's grid capacity and residential affordability.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Investors sizing data center capex should factor in that even 'smaller' 50MW projects compete directly with residential load, inviting local political and regulatory friction.
insights.quote: even on the somewhat smaller side, 50MW of power required, that's 50,000 homes

insights.claim: Under New York's forthcoming data center framework, developers will have to either bring their own power generation source or pay a premium to draw from the state grid, with the Public Service Commission finalizing the rules within the one-year moratorium.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: A bring-your-own-power-or-pay-premium model raises the effective cost of siting in New York, favoring developers who can co-locate generation (e.g., gas, SMRs) over pure grid-draw projects.
insights.quote: you come, you're the bring your own power source or you have to pay a premium to use our grid.

insights.claim: Hochul says data center companies have been willing to pay rural host communities on the order of $1 million per megawatt, but many small upstate localities lacked the planning staff and sophistication to negotiate such terms, a gap the state's new community investment framework and grid resiliency fund are meant to close.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Community payment benchmarks (~$1M/MW) are becoming a de facto price of entry for data center siting deals, raising project costs and local-approval complexity nationally.
insights.quote: we want you to give, you know, $1 million per megawatt

insights.claim: Even amid national anti-data-center political backlash, New York continues to offer tax breaks to attract data center investment, showing that 'compute scarcity' narratives coexist with active interstate competition for these facilities.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal, host
insights.so_what: States are simultaneously restricting and subsidizing data centers, so investors should track state-by-state policy divergence rather than assume uniform national headwinds.

insights.claim: Facing federal opposition to renewable energy and surging AI-driven power demand, Governor Hochul is pursuing an 'all of the above' energy strategy for New York combining Quebec hydro imports, offshore wind, a new Pennsylvania-to-Long Island natural gas pipeline, and nuclear expansion.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: New York's multi-source buildout creates investment opportunities across hydro transmission, offshore wind, gas pipeline, and nuclear supply chains simultaneously rather than a single-technology bet.

insights.claim: Hochul says she wants to build more nuclear power capacity in New York than the entire United States has built in the past 30 years, and is negotiating with the U.S. Department of Energy for long-term, low-interest loans to fund new reactors at existing upstate sites.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: A state-level nuclear buildout of this scale, if realized, would be a major catalyst for nuclear equipment, fuel, and construction-trade suppliers, contingent on federal DOE loan approval.
insights.quote: I want to build more nuclear in New York than the entire country has in 30 years.

insights.claim: Hochul calls the 2021 shutdown of the Indian Point nuclear plant, which once supplied 20-25% of New York City's power, one of the biggest mistakes in state history, and says restarting it is now complicated because the plant has already been decommissioned and had its radioactive materials removed, despite the owner's claim a restart is 'technically doable.'
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: A quick Indian Point restart is likely off the table as a near-term power source for NYC-area AI/data-center demand, despite periodic headlines suggesting otherwise.
insights.quote: one of the biggest mistakes in the state's history was shutting down Indian Point, which powered 20 to 25% of New York City's power without a plan B

insights.claim: To partially offset Indian Point's lost capacity, Hochul authorized construction of a hydroelectric transmission line importing power from Quebec within weeks of taking office, which she describes as the longest underground/underwater transmission line in the country.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Cross-border hydro transmission infrastructure is a multi-year, capital-intensive bet; investors in grid/transmission construction and Canadian hydro exporters should track New York-specific opportunity here.

insights.claim: New York is also developing an offshore wind facility Hochul says will power roughly half a million homes in Brooklyn, which she is fighting to keep alive against federal opposition she calls a 'war on offshore wind.'
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Offshore wind project completion in New York remains politically contested at the federal level, adding permitting and timeline risk for offshore wind developers and suppliers.
insights.quote: a facility that'll power half a million homes in Brooklyn

insights.claim: Hochul is offering a direct incentive for power-hungry AI and semiconductor projects: any company that brings its own small modular reactor (SMR) capacity will be granted site access, signaling the state wants to become a hub for SMR deployment.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: SMR developers and manufacturers have a concrete state-level customer pathway in New York, tied directly to large industrial and data-center siting decisions.
insights.quote: Data centers or the next micron show up with your own small modular reactor capability, and I'll find the space for you.

insights.claim: Hochul says nuclear plant approvals typically take about ten years, seven of them at the federal level, and that she has asked the Trump administration to streamline federal review while she speeds up state approvals, one of the few areas where she describes alignment with Washington, in contrast to friction over offshore wind, Canadian-material tariffs, and ICE enforcement.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Federal permitting reform is the binding constraint on New York's nuclear timeline; DOE/administration signals on streamlining are a leading indicator for how fast new capacity can come online.
insights.quote: It takes ten years for approvals. So seven of those years are at the federal level.

insights.claim: New York secured a roughly $100 billion Micron semiconductor investment, which Hochul calls the largest private-sector investment in American history, expected to create 10,000 direct and 40,000 indirect jobs, with the state committing to secure the plant's power supply through the New York Power Authority.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: The Micron buildout is a multi-decade capex and job-creation anchor for upstate New York, with state-guaranteed power supply reducing project execution risk versus data centers stuck in the moratorium queue.
insights.quote: Largest private sector investment in American history.

insights.claim: Given a choice, Hochul says she would prioritize allocating scarce grid power to a jobs-heavy manufacturer like Micron over a 'largely vacant' data center consuming the same amount of electricity, revealing the implicit hierarchy of who gets power access under the moratorium regime.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Data center developers should expect to be second in line for New York grid capacity behind job-creating manufacturing investments like chip fabs, affecting siting and interconnection timelines.
insights.quote: if I had to choose between powering a largely vacant data center with the same amount of power, I can have a micron with thousand jobs

insights.claim: To meet Micron's stated need for 9,000 engineers and tens of thousands of construction-trade workers, New York has already rewritten school curricula in the nine counties surrounding the Micron site to teach relevant skills starting in kindergarten, a roughly 30-year workforce pipeline.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Long-lead workforce investment signals the state sees Micron as a multi-decade anchor tenant, relevant for semiconductor supply-chain investors evaluating the project's durability.
insights.quote: Micron, when I was trying to recruit them, said they needed 9000 engineers and tens of thousands of people in the construction trades.

insights.claim: Hochul launched a 'Future Works Commission' bringing together business, labor, and academic leaders to study AI's impact on jobs and design reskilling programs, explicitly citing her memory of automation and offshoring hollowing out Bethlehem Steel-era Buffalo as the reason for acting pre-emptively rather than reactively.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: State-level AI labor policy is moving from rhetoric to institution-building; companies operating in New York should expect emerging reskilling/consortium expectations rather than new direct taxes.
insights.quote: We are now called the Future Works Commission.

insights.claim: Hochul says she gathered ten New Yorkers in her office who had recently lost jobs to AI, including people who had studied computer science and coding, and notes that women are disproportionately concentrated in the roles she considers most vulnerable to AI displacement.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Even technically-skilled workers with CS/coding backgrounds are reporting AI-driven job loss, undercutting the assumption that STEM training alone insulates workers from AI disruption.
insights.quote: I gathered ten of them in my office the other day. I invited people who had lost their jobs.

insights.claim: Rather than directly taxing tech companies to fund worker retraining, Hochul is pushing a 'consortium' model where AI firms contribute expertise and technology, such as helping data centers cut energy, water, and noise footprints, in exchange for New York's continued low-tax stance toward high earners and businesses.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: AI and data center companies operating in New York face soft-power pressure (in-kind contributions, technical partnerships) rather than new tax liabilities, differing from labor-disruption funding models proposed elsewhere.

themes.theme: Waymo and the Politics of Concentrated Losers

insights.claim: New York paused Waymo's driverless-car trial because Hochul wants a concrete transition plan ('Plan B') for professional drivers before allowing full rollout, drawing an explicit parallel to the lack of worker-transition support during the Industrial Revolution and prior automation waves.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Autonomous vehicle operators like Waymo face state-level political risk in New York tied not to safety data but to displaced-worker transition planning, a distinct regulatory hurdle for AV rollout timelines.
insights.quote: What is the alternative for the people who now are the ones we're counting on to drive us in person? You have to have a plan B.

insights.claim: Odd Lots hosts push back that the number of people who would benefit as passengers from driverless cars is far larger than the number of professional drivers who would be displaced, arguing New York's Waymo pause prioritizes a smaller, concentrated group over larger diffuse public benefits like safety and reduced congestion.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal, host
insights.so_what: The cost-benefit framing used against AV rollouts (concentrated losers vs. diffuse winners) is a recurring political pattern that could apply to other AI-driven automation debates, useful for handicapping regulatory risk.
insights.quote: like the number of people who would theoretically be a passenger in any car is far larger than the number of people who drive cars professionally.

insights.claim: Odd Lots hosts frame New York's driverless-car caution as a 'Jones Act phenomenon,' where a small, identifiable group with concentrated losses (professional drivers) can delay or block policies with large diffuse benefits, and suggest this dynamic is likely to recur across many future AI labor disruptions, making Waymo's New York trajectory an early test case worth watching.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal, host
insights.so_what: Investors and operators in AI-exposed industries should watch for 'Jones Act'-style political blocking by concentrated loser groups as a template risk factor, independent of the underlying technology's actual safety or efficiency case.
insights.quote: This is the Jones act phenomenon which is like how many people have Jones act jobs

themes.theme: Using AI to Deregulate New York's Rulebook

insights.claim: Hochul used AI to scan New York's entire regulatory and legal code for outdated or antiquated rules, generating more than 4,000 public recommendations for repeal in a couple of months, a review she says would have taken five years using state staff alone, and has directed all state agencies to act on the flagged list.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: AI-driven regulatory review is a concrete, replicable government-efficiency use case with a measurable speed-up (5 years to ~2 months), relevant to govtech vendors and other jurisdictions considering similar deregulation sprints.
insights.quote: I would have tried taking five years at the staff level in a couple of months, and I have over 4000 recommendations from the public who want us to look into things.

insights.claim: Examples of antiquated New York rules flagged by the AI regulatory review include a requirement that pregnant women obtain a special permit to work after midnight and a $25 fee to bring a dog hunting in the state.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Illustrates the kind of low-value, low-risk compliance burden AI-assisted review can identify and clear quickly, a template for cost-of-doing-business improvements in other jurisdictions.
insights.quote: You have to pay $25 to bring your dog hunting in the state

insights.claim: Odd Lots hosts frame the AI regulatory scan as a template AI use case, reducing an unreviewable corpus of millions of pages of code into a manageable shortlist of flagged items for human judgment, rather than replacing human review outright.
insights.speaker: Tracy Alloway, host
insights.so_what: The 'AI narrows, humans decide' pattern demonstrated here is a reusable model for applying AI to large unstructured compliance and legal corpora across government and regulated industries.
insights.quote: find the dumb stuff

themes.theme: Finance Competition and Housing Affordability Politics

insights.claim: Despite JPMorgan expanding in Texas and Citadel expanding in Florida, Hochul says New York City commercial office leasing has hit extraordinary levels over the past three to four months, citing American Express's new World Trade Center headquarters as a 'statement of confidence' that finance and tech firms are expanding rather than relocating away from New York.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: New York office and commercial real estate leasing strength contradicts the narrative of wholesale finance-sector flight to Texas and Florida, relevant for NYC office REIT and commercial real estate positioning.
insights.quote: There's only one New York.

insights.claim: Hochul links the rise of Democratic Socialist politics in New York City, including Zoran Mamdani's recent electoral wins, directly to housing unaffordability, citing that the average age of a first-time homebuyer rose from 30 to 40 within just a few years.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: Housing affordability is the proximate driver of NYC's leftward political shift per the sitting governor's own diagnosis, suggesting housing policy is the key variable to watch for the city's political trajectory.
insights.quote: the average age of a first time home buyer, if you're lucky enough to have a home at all, went from 30 to 40

insights.claim: To unblock housing supply, Hochul pushed through a reform of New York's 1975 SEQR environmental review law this year despite it being called 'dead on arrival' in the legislature, and describes ongoing NIMBY resistance, including from self-identified progressive city council members, to office-to-residential conversions and a low-income homeownership project for 900 people.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: SEQR reform is a concrete, already-enacted deregulatory lever for New York housing supply this year; investors in NYC residential development and office-conversion plays should track its implementation and continued local-level resistance.
insights.quote: it was called dead on arrival

insights.claim: Hochul is funding a $1 billion energy rebate program to offset New Yorkers' utility bills and says she took on trial lawyers to reduce auto insurance costs, positioning cost-of-living relief as her top political priority alongside energy policy.
insights.speaker: Kathy Hochul, NY Governor
insights.so_what: A $1 billion rebate program is a direct fiscal offset to rising electricity costs partly driven by AI and data center demand, relevant to utilities and ratepayer-cost analysis in New York.
insights.quote: I'm going to do $1 billion energy rebate to help pay utility bills.
